responses.strategic_rationale: Denmark's digital identity system faces structural supplier concentration risk through its dependence on Apple and Google platform integrity signals, device trust, and app distribution channels. The project must navigate three competing tensions: (1) technical feasibility of platform-neutral authentication without abandoning eIDAS2 compliance and institutional trust frameworks; (2) political viability of shifting procurement requirements before identity infrastructure hardens around current dependencies; and (3) resource constraints that demand focused intervention at AltID and standards bodies rather than attempting production-grade MitID replacement. The selected levers address these tensions by sequencing coalition-building and credible technical evidence (phases 1–2) ahead of procurement intervention (phase 3), while maintaining institutional engagement pathways and EU alignment to overcome incumbent resistance and regulatory friction.

levers.name: Coalition Composition and Advocacy Reach
levers.consequences: The breadth and credibility of the coalition—spanning civil society, academic researchers, security experts, contingency planners, and parliamentary allies—determines whether platform-neutrality framing gains traction as a resilience issue or remains marginal technical criticism. A coalition that includes privacy advocates and procurement-transparency organizations can reframe the dependency as a public-sector risk rather than ideology, but coalition-building during phase one competes for limited engagement capacity and may absorb political capital that later procurement work requires.
options: Recruit a cross-sector coalition (NGOs, academia, security researchers, parliamentary members) early in phase one, with explicit governance and public-facing communications to amplify resilience framing before institutional actors entrench positions.

Prioritize deep relationships with two or three anchor institutions (one government agency, one university, one civil-society organization) and let organic network growth follow, accepting slower reach but reducing coordination overhead and dependency on volunteer engagement.

Build a coalition primarily through a formally constituted steering committee of government procurement and digital-identity specialists, reducing external stakeholder involvement but increasing institutional legitimacy and direct procurement influence over time.
levers.review_lever: Early coalition visibility risks signaling intent to incumbent platform vendors and MitID operators, potentially triggering institutional defensiveness or delays in phase-two technical partnerships. The options assume coalition members will sustain engagement across 24 months without explicit incentive structures or decision-making power, but parliamentary and NGO stakeholders may deprioritize the issue if competing digital-policy agendas emerge.

levers.name: Demonstrator Technical Scope and Standards Choice
levers.consequences: The demonstrator choice between FIDO2/WebAuthn plus OpenID Connect on GrapheneOS versus a browser-based or hardware-token fallback path determines both technical credibility and institutional acceptance. FIDO2 has broader standards recognition but remains tied to mobile OS ecosystems; hardware tokens offer genuine platform independence but introduce usability and deployment friction that procurement stakeholders may view as impractical for public-sector citizen access.
options: Develop the primary demonstrator using FIDO2/WebAuthn on GrapheneOS with a secondary hardware-token fallback, positioning mobile-first access as the main pathway and hardware tokens as a continuity reserve for critical scenarios where platform policy changes block app access.

Reverse priority and develop the hardware-token demonstrator first, using it as proof that citizen authentication can function entirely outside the Apple/Google ecosystem, then add a secondary mobile demonstrator as an ergonomic enhancement rather than the core trust path.

Build two equally resourced demonstrators in parallel—one FIDO2/WebAuthn mobile path and one browser-based fallback using server-side authentication and session persistence—to avoid committing to a single standards approach and to test institutional appetite for each before phase three procurement work begins.
levers.review_lever: Demonstrators must convince procurement teams that platform-neutral access does not impose unacceptable usability costs on citizens, yet all three options assume that a non-production proof-of-concept will sufficiently lower institutional risk perception. None of the options account for the likelihood that procurers will demand production hardening, liability frameworks, and carrier or banking-sector certification before considering alternatives to proven MitID infrastructure.

levers.name: Regulatory Pathway and eIDAS2 Alignment
levers.consequences: The project's ability to shape AltID and future procurement depends on establishing clear eIDAS2 Substantial-level compliance and NSIS conformance for platform-neutral alternatives before procurement specifications freeze. Early regulatory clarity increases institutional confidence but narrows design space; delayed regulatory engagement allows technical flexibility but risks arriving at phase three with no clear path to formal compliance, leaving procurers with no grounds to require platform-neutral alternatives.
options: Engage Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and EU Digital Identity Regulation working groups in phase one, establishing formal regulatory positions and gaining early feedback on eIDAS2 conformance barriers, accepting slower technical iteration but building institutional confidence that platform-neutral designs can meet certification requirements.

Defer regulatory engagement until phase two is complete, allowing demonstrators to inform realistic compliance requirements and risk assessment, then use evidence from working prototypes to negotiate regulatory positions with government bodies in phase three with greater technical certainty.

Pursue parallel engagement with both Danish national authorities and European Commission technical working groups from phase one onward, positioning the project as an EU-level standards contribution rather than a national procurement intervention, which broadens influence but dilutes focus on Danish AltID outcomes.
levers.review_lever: eIDAS2 certification timelines and approval gates are outside the project's control, and regulatory bodies may not formally respond to platform-neutrality requirements until broader European Digital Identity Wallet rules solidify. The options assume that regulatory engagement will yield cooperative movement toward platform-neutral certification requirements, but incumbent operators and conservative certification bodies may argue that splitting the trust model between platform-dependent and platform-neutral paths introduces unmanageable assurance complexity.

levers.name: Incumbent Operator Engagement and Non-Cooperation Risk
levers.consequences: MitID's operator and Apple/Google relationships represent structural barriers to phase-two demonstrator credibility and phase-three procurement leverage. Constructive engagement with MitID operators could provide technical clarification and institutional legitimacy, but risks becoming co-opted into incremental improvements that do not address platform dependency, or facing deliberate obstruction if operators perceive the project as a threat to their commercial position.
options: Seek formal technical partnership with the MitID operator in phase one, framing the demonstrator work as a continuity-planning exercise and fallback capability that complements rather than competes with MitID, accepting risk of co-optation but gaining access to technical specifications and institutional credibility.

Treat MitID operators as a stakeholder constituency to be informed rather than engaged, keeping demonstrator work technically independent and transparent, publishing findings openly to create institutional pressure for platform-neutral design without seeking operator approval or partnership.

Establish a formal non-cooperation framework from phase one, with clear technical and communication boundaries, acknowledging that operators may not cooperate constructively and structuring work to avoid dependence on operator consent or technical disclosure, at the cost of reduced institutional legitimacy with some government bodies.
levers.review_lever: MitID operators have strong incentives to maintain current platform dependencies and may interpret demonstrator work as an implicit criticism of existing infrastructure. The options assume the project can succeed with operator non-cooperation or lukewarm cooperation, but if procurers view the MitID operator's assessment of fallback-authentication risks as authoritative, the project's recommendations may be dismissed as technically unsound or operationally unfeasible regardless of demonstrator evidence.

levers.name: Procurement Intervention Timing and AltID Engagement Window
levers.consequences: AltID rollout timeline and procurement specification windows narrow as planning moves from phase one (months 1–8) into phase two and three. Early procurement engagement increases influence but risks overcommitting before demonstrators and feasibility reports are ready; late engagement preserves technical credibility but may arrive after critical architecture decisions have been locked in by institutional inertia or vendor planning cycles.
options: Initiate formal procurement engagement in parallel with phase-one research output (month 6–8), presenting the feasibility report and coalition position to AltID governance as input to ongoing design consultation, accepting risk of incomplete technical evidence but capturing the planning window before specifications freeze.

Delay major procurement outreach until phase-two demonstrators are complete (month 14–16), using working prototypes and documented standards compliance to support platform-neutrality requirements during AltID procurement drafting, trading earlier influence for stronger technical leverage.

Maintain continuous low-level procurement engagement throughout phases one and two (quarterly briefings, working-group participation) rather than concentrated pushes, distributing influence across the planning cycle but diffusing institutional attention and requiring sustained coalition energy.
levers.review_lever: AltID procurement timelines are set by government digital transformation roadmaps, not by this project, and institutional planning horizons may already be committed to platform-dependent architecture. The options assume that procurement teams will be receptive to fallback-authentication requirements once technical feasibility is demonstrated, but if competing priorities (cost control, rapid deployment, incumbent vendor relationships) dominate procurement decisions, the project's evidence will have minimal effect on final specifications.

levers.name: EU Standards Contribution versus Danish-Focused Outcome
levers.consequences: Phase-three EU engagement through European Digital Identity Wallet working groups and standards bodies can amplify platform-neutrality language at a regulatory level, potentially influencing broader eIDAS2 implementation across member states and creating downstream pressure on Danish procurement. However, EU-level advocacy dilutes focus on Danish AltID outcomes and introduces dependency on other member states' priorities, timelines, and political will; the project may gain EU-level influence while failing to secure platform-neutrality requirements in actual Danish procurement.
options: Prioritize EU-level engagement as a phase-three strategy, framing platform-neutrality as a member-state resilience and digital-sovereignty issue within broader European Digital Identity Wallet discussions, with the goal of securing EU-level language that later constrains or encourages Danish procurement decisions.

Keep EU engagement subordinate to Danish procurement focus, using European working groups as a forum for validating technical work and positioning Denmark as a resilience leader, but concentrating primary effort on securing platform-neutrality requirements in AltID rather than pursuing European regulatory influence.

Pursue parallel Danish and EU tracks from phase one, allocating resources to both procurement intervention and standards contribution, accepting diluted focus but positioning the project as a bridge between national digital sovereignty concerns and European digital-identity harmonization.
levers.review_lever: European Digital Identity Wallet standards and member-state adoption timelines are not synchronized with Danish AltID deployment, and platform-neutrality language at the EU level does not automatically translate into binding procurement requirements for member states. The options assume that EU-level engagement will eventually reinforce Danish procurement decisions, but EU standards bodies may prioritize interoperability and wallet functionality over supplier concentration concerns, leaving platform-neutrality language as aspirational rather than mandatory.

levers.name: Success Measurement and Phase-Three De-Scoping Conditions
levers.consequences: The project defines success through institutional outcomes (formal AltID inclusion of platform-neutrality language) and published evidence, not through consumer adoption or production deployment. If phase-three procurement engagement yields weak institutional traction by month 16, the project plan anticipates de-scoping toward publication and coalition consolidation rather than forcing procurement influence. This disciplined exit prevents resource waste but risks communicating that platform-neutrality requirements are optional and creates perception of failure if AltID does not formally adopt the project's recommendations.
options: Establish a hard go/no-go decision point at month 16, assessing whether procurement stakeholders have demonstrated genuine engagement with platform-neutrality proposals; if engagement is weak, de-scope phase three toward publication and EU standards work, accepting that AltID may not be directly influenced but preserving project credibility and evidence contribution.

Front-load success criteria into phases one and two (feasibility report publication, demonstrator delivery, formal institutional response), treating phase-three procurement outcomes as contingent on external conditions beyond the project's control, reducing expectation that procurement adoption is guaranteed and focusing instead on deliverable evidence.

Expand phase-three success criteria beyond AltID to include secondary institutional influences (banking-sector adoption of fallback authentication, regional government procurement alignment, parliamentary digital-policy committee endorsement), broadening the definition of success to reduce dependence on a single procurement outcome.
levers.review_lever: AltID procurement decisions are driven by government digital transformation budgets, vendor relationships, and deployment timelines that predate this project and may be largely locked in by phase three. The options assume that institutional decision-makers will be persuaded by feasibility reports and demonstrators to revise procurement specifications toward platform-neutrality, but procurement momentum and incumbent vendor contracts may make significant architecture changes politically and budgetarily infeasible regardless of technical evidence presented.

responses.strategic_rationale: Denmark's digital identity system faces structural vendor lock-in through Apple and Google's control over app distribution, device-integrity signals, and platform telemetry. The project must navigate three competing pressures: (1) technical credibility—demonstrators must prove platform-neutral alternatives are viable without appearing academic or disconnected from real constraints; (2) institutional capture—incumbent operators (MitID, telecom providers) benefit from the status quo and will resist procurement language that threatens their current trust model; and (3) political window—AltID rollout and EU eIDAS2 harmonization create a 24-month intervention window before the next identity stack hardens the same dependencies. The selected levers focus on sequencing technical proof, coalition leverage, and procurement intervention to maximize the probability that supplier-concentration language enters official AltID and EU Digital Identity Wallet materials before incumbent resistance calcifies.

levers.name: Demonstrator Credibility Architecture and Integration Depth
levers.consequences: Choosing how deeply the demonstrators integrate with real Danish authentication infrastructure (simulated MitID environment, live Digitaliseringsstyrelsen test systems, or purely standalone) determines both technical credibility and political safety. Deeper integration (test-system access) produces stronger proof-of-concept but risks operational friction with incumbent operators or demands early regulatory approval that may stall the project. Standalone demonstrators preserve control and speed but invite dismissal as academically interesting but operationally detached.
options: Build demonstrators against fully simulated Danish public-sector identity-provider infrastructure without requesting live test-system access, ensuring technical autonomy and avoiding early escalation to MitID or Digitaliseringsstyrelsen gatekeepers.

Negotiate limited read-only or sandboxed access to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen's eIDAS2 test environment by month 12, framing the request as validation of inter-standard compatibility rather than as an implicit threat to MitID.

Partner with a sympathetic regional municipality or public agency to conduct a small-scale pilot authentication workflow using the demonstrator against real public-sector backend systems, creating operational legitimacy while maintaining distance from national identity operators.
levers.review_lever: Deeper integration with real systems strengthens the project's legitimacy as a governance and procurement intervention, but none of the options account for the timeline risk: regulatory approvals for test-system access can stretch 4–8 months, compressing phase-two technical delivery. The fallback option (municipal pilot) avoids central gatekeepers but creates a new dependency on local political appetite and may expose the project to early incumbent pushback if MitID operators learn of parallel authentication testing in the public sector.

levers.name: Standards Contribution versus Standards Avoidance
levers.consequences: Engaging actively with EU technical working groups (ETSI, eIDAS2 revision bodies) on platform neutrality language enhances the project's influence on European Digital Identity Wallet specifications and creates political cover for Danish procurement intervention. Active engagement also accelerates learning on emerging interoperability standards and potential regulatory tailwinds. Conversely, high-profile standards involvement raises the project's visibility to incumbent operators earlier and may invite competitive or defensive responses from platform vendors and their allies in telecom and banking sectors.
options: Contribute formal position papers and technical input to ETSI and eIDAS2 working groups starting in phase two, positioning the project as an independent voice advocating for platform-neutral and resilience-oriented language in emerging European standards.

Engage with EU bodies only after phase two demonstrators are complete and polished, maximizing technical credibility before entering high-stakes standards discussions and reducing the risk of premature incumbent opposition.

Defer direct standards engagement and instead publish peer-reviewed academic papers on platform-neutral Danish identity architecture in open journals and conference proceedings, allowing the project to influence standards conversations through scholarly authority rather than institutional advocacy.
levers.review_lever: Early standards engagement amplifies the project's leverage but creates visibility risk: incumbent operators actively monitor eIDAS2 and European Digital Identity Wallet working groups, and early disclosure of platform-neutrality arguments may trigger organized counter-arguments or regulatory pressure. The academic-publications strategy delays visibility but also delays influence—by the time papers are published and cited in standards discussions, AltID procurement may already be locked into Apple and Google dependencies, making the European standards window less relevant to Danish implementation.

levers.name: Coalition Widening and Veto-Player Management
levers.consequences: Expanding the coalition beyond civil-society and academic stakeholders to include sympathetic voices within Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the banking sector, or the telecom industry magnifies institutional influence but risks compromising the project's framing if commercial partners demand messaging that dilutes the sovereignty and resilience narrative. Narrower coalitions preserve clarity and independence but reduce access to decision-makers and implementation capacity.
options: Build a coalition exclusively with civil-society organisations, privacy advocates, academic researchers, and security experts, maintaining editorial control over messaging and avoiding entanglement with commercial interests that profit from the current MitID ecosystem.

Actively recruit banking and fintech leaders who face operational constraints under Apple/Google payment-system policies, positioning platform neutrality as a commercial-resilience issue that benefits the private sector alongside public digital sovereignty.

Identify and cultivate relationships with procurement officers, IT security leads, and business-continuity planners within Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and major public-sector agencies, framing platform neutrality as an operational and contingency-planning imperative independent of policy ideology.
levers.review_lever: Including commercial partners (banking, fintech) broadens the political base for procurement intervention and adds credibility on operational constraints, but none of the options anticipate the conflict-of-interest dynamics: banks benefit from Apple/Google dominance for payment-system lock-in, and fintech firms often depend on platform app stores for distribution. A coalition that includes both digital-sovereignty advocates and commercial partners risks internal credibility loss if these actors' interests diverge during the critical AltID procurement negotiation window.

levers.name: Fallback Mechanism Design Philosophy and Trust Model
levers.consequences: Choosing the fallback authentication path—browser-based, hardware-token, or a hybrid approach reducing but not eliminating mobile OS dependence—shapes both technical feasibility and the strength of the governance argument. Browser-based fallback avoids OS lock-in but requires infrastructure (government-hosted authentication endpoints) that may face resistance from MitID operators. Hardware tokens are resilient but introduce cost and UX barriers to citizen adoption. The choice also determines whether the fallback is truly platform-neutral or merely shifts dependence to a different vendor ecosystem.
options: Design the fallback as a browser-based OpenID Connect workflow integrated with a government-operated digital identity provider, eliminating dependence on mobile app stores and OS-level integrity signals but requiring new public-sector infrastructure and security certification.

Develop a hardware-token fallback using open standards such as FIDO2 and USB or NFC interfaces, providing strong platform neutrality and resilience but introducing cost, logistics, and citizen-literacy barriers that may limit practical adoption in a national-scale identity system.

Create a hybrid fallback combining a privacy-focused Android alternative (GrapheneOS) with WebAuthn for authentication, accepting residual OS-level dependence on the Android ecosystem while reducing the degree of platform vendor lock-in and avoiding government infrastructure expansion.
levers.review_lever: Browser-based fallback provides the cleanest platform neutrality but requires government to operate a new authentication infrastructure with its own security and liability implications, creating an institutional burden that Digitaliseringsstyrelsen may resist. Hardware-token fallback maximizes resilience but the usability and distribution costs create a political opening for incumbent operators to argue that platform neutrality sacrifices citizen convenience. The hybrid Android-based approach sidesteps both infrastructure and usability problems but weakens the sovereignty argument—shifting from Apple/Google lock-in to Android/Google lock-in is a partial solution that may not satisfy the political motivation for reduced foreign vendor dependence.

levers.name: AltID Engagement Sequencing and Pressure Application
levers.consequences: Timing intervention in the AltID design and procurement process determines whether the project can influence requirements before institutional momentum hardens. Early engagement (months 9–12) allows input into initial design discussions but risks appearing premature if demonstrators are incomplete. Late engagement (months 18–20) ensures technical credibility but collides with final procurement decisions when flexibility is minimal. The choice also determines whether the project frames itself as a collaborative design partner or as an external pressure and standards advocate.
options: Request formal technical review slots in the official AltID design working groups by month 10, presenting demonstrator prototypes and feasibility findings as design inputs and framing the project as constructive technical contributor to the AltID roadmap.

Withhold direct AltID engagement until month 16–18 when demonstrators are complete and polished, then deliver a comprehensive technical and policy report to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and publicly, using peer-reviewed credibility and external validation to create procurement pressure at the critical decision gate.

Maintain informal advisory relationships with sympathetic AltID stakeholders throughout the project, gathering intelligence on design decisions and procurement timelines while building the coalition, then deploy concentrated advocacy and technical evidence at inflection points when procurement language is actually being drafted.
levers.review_lever: Early collaborative engagement offers influence but depends on AltID leadership's openness to platform-neutrality messaging—if institutional positions are already set, early requests for design review are routed to technical committees and deprioritized. Late high-profile intervention maximizes technical credibility but may arrive after procurement language is drafted, limiting ability to shape requirements. The informal advisory approach preserves flexibility and coalition-building time but creates information asymmetry: the project may not detect procurement milestones until they have already passed or are committed in non-public stakeholder agreements.

levers.name: Regulatory Pathway Clarity and eIDAS2 Compliance Framing
levers.consequences: Explicitly stating that platform-neutral and fallback-capable systems can meet eIDAS2 Substantial-level assurance requirements reframes the issue from 'regulatory burden' to 'regulatory permission,' lowering political resistance. Producing detailed compliance evidence (gap analyses, threat models, test procedures) makes it harder for incumbent operators to argue that platform neutrality creates unacceptable assurance gaps. However, this reframing also invites detailed regulatory scrutiny and may trigger formal consultations with supervisory bodies, extending timelines and introducing new veto points.
options: Commission an independent legal and technical analysis of eIDAS2 and NSIS Substantial-level assurance requirements, documenting specific regulatory pathways through which platform-neutral and fallback authentication architectures can demonstrate equivalent or superior security posture compared to current Apple/Google-mediated trust models.

Work with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen's compliance and standards teams to informally discuss eIDAS2 interpretations relevant to platform neutrality, building internal regulatory confidence without triggering formal consultation processes or creating paper trails that incumbent operators can contest.

Publish technical threat models and security analyses demonstrating that platform-neutral fallback mechanisms reduce certain systemic risks (single points of failure, geopolitical vendor lock-in, app-store distribution vulnerabilities) relative to the current architecture, reframing the compliance conversation around resilience rather than equivalence.
levers.review_lever: Formal independent compliance analysis is the most credible approach but risks regulatory capture: if the analyst is perceived as aligned with the project's advocacy goals, incumbent operators will challenge the findings as biased. Informal regulatory discussions with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen preserve speed and flexibility but create no formal record or institutional commitment, allowing regulators to deny or reinterpret earlier conversations when procurement disputes arise. Published threat models shift the conversation away from equivalence—a favorable move—but without formal eIDAS2 guidance, threat-model framing can be dismissed as theoretical rather than operationally binding.

levers.name: Phase-Three Contingency and De-Scoping Conditions
levers.consequences: Explicitly defining the conditions under which phase three pivots from AltID procurement intervention toward EU standards and coalition consolidation preserves project credibility and prevents sunk-cost escalation. If formal AltID traction is weak by month 16, shifting to publication and EU-facing standards work is politically honest but reduces immediate Danish procurement impact. Conversely, committing to AltID intervention regardless of institutional receptiveness risks phase-three budget burn and project-credibility damage if the final outcome is symbolic language without procurement teeth.
options: Establish clear phase-three go/no-go criteria by month 15: if Digitaliseringsstyrelsen has not formally incorporated platform-neutrality or fallback-access language into official AltID design documents or procurement briefs, the project automatically de-scopes toward academic publication, EU standards contribution, and coalition archive; reallocate remaining phase-three budget (approximately 1.5 million DKK) toward long-term advocacy infrastructure and follow-on funding applications.

Negotiate explicit outcome commitments from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and the AltID program leadership by month 12, formalizing written agreements on how demonstrator findings and policy recommendations will feed into procurement requirements; if commitments are not honored by month 18, trigger a public-stakeholder review and media engagement strategy to escalate pressure before final AltID decisions.

Preserve phase-three optionality by maintaining active AltID engagement and coalition-building throughout the project without pre-committing to institutional success criteria, allowing the project leadership to make month-16 decisions based on actual political conditions and demonstrator reception rather than pre-set conditions.
levers.review_lever: Clear go/no-go criteria provide disciplined risk management and preserve credibility, but the automatic de-scope option risks appearing as project failure to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and future funders, even if EU standards and coalition outcomes are substantial. Negotiated outcome commitments create formal accountability and prevent mission creep, but Digitaliseringsstyrelsen will resist binding language that constrains their AltID flexibility. Preserving optionality allows adaptive decision-making at month 16 but defers the hardest institutional and financial decisions to a point when demonstrators are complete and political positions have hardened, potentially trapping the project between competing advocacy strategies with insufficient budget to execute all of them credibly.

responses.strategic_rationale: Denmark's digital-identity system faces acute supplier-concentration risk: MitID's trust model depends on Apple and Google platform signals for app integrity and distribution, leaving critical public services vulnerable to policy shifts or geopolitical disruption. The project intervenes at three pressure points — technical demonstration of viable alternatives, procurement and standards influence around the AltID wallet rollout, and coalition-based advocacy framing resilience and continuity rather than ideology. The most critical levers are those that shift the conversation from abstract sovereignty to concrete continuity-planning risk, that create credible technical proof-of-concept without claiming production readiness, and that embed platform-neutrality language in procurement and EU standards before the next identity stack locks in the same dependencies. These seven levers navigate the core tension between ambitious technical scope and realistic political influence: overreaching on the demonstrator kills credibility; undershooting on procurement language wastes the window.

levers.name: Trust Anchor Migration and Fallback Authentication Binding
levers.consequences: Shifting which entity (MitID operator, public identity provider, or a hybrid model) certifies and manages the fallback authentication path determines both technical feasibility and institutional acceptance. If MitID retains control, the fallback remains within the existing trust envelope but may preserve dependencies; if a public identity provider assumes certification, it creates a parallel trust path but requires new institutional coordination and legal frameworks.
options: Propose that the Danish public identity provider (not MitID operator) takes responsibility for certifying a platform-neutral fallback path, accepting institutional friction but creating a genuinely independent authentication route separate from the app-ecosystem mediation.

Design a hybrid trust model where MitID retains primary certification for mainstream access but explicitly delegates fallback or continuity authentication to a separately governed, standards-aligned identity service that uses open protocols.

Architect a distributed trust model using cryptographic proof-of-work or threshold signatures to reduce reliance on any single institutional certifier, transferring the trust anchor from vendor or operator to distributed ledger infrastructure or multi-party computation.
levers.review_lever: Shifting trust anchors solves platform dependency on paper, but the core tension is institutional: whoever certifies the fallback path must be trusted by the entire ecosystem (banks, tax authority, healthcare systems) and must be perceived as neutral and competent. None of the options address how to bootstrap trust in a new certifying body that has no track record managing production authentication for critical services. Additionally, if the fallback path is rarely used, institutional will to maintain it atrophies over time.

levers.name: Demonstrator Integration Depth and Production Proximity
levers.consequences: Deciding how closely the phase-two demonstrators mirror real MitID architecture and live public-sector identity providers affects credibility and risk exposure. Deeper integration (test signing with real identity-provider certificates, integration with actual banking sandboxes) generates stronger proof-of-concept but increases regulatory oversight and incident liability; shallow integration (purely simulated environments, synthetic data) remains defensible but is more easily dismissed as academic.
options: Build demonstrators using non-production sandbox credentials issued specifically by Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or MitID operator for this project, integrating with real identity-provider test systems so that the proof-of-concept is validated against live institutional infrastructure without touching production authentication.

Construct entirely simulated identity-provider environments using open-standards reference implementations, ensuring the demonstrators are fully self-contained and cannot be mistaken for production systems, prioritizing legal and regulatory clarity over integration depth.

Develop a staged demonstrator pathway where the mobile and fallback authenticators begin in simulation but include architectural hooks and documented migration paths that could allow incremental integration with real public-sector systems after phase two, signaling scalability without immediate production coupling.
levers.review_lever: Integration depth trades off credibility against complexity and liability. Deeper integration strengthens the message that platform-neutral authentication is technically viable, but it also raises institutional concerns about whether the project is attempting a stealth product deployment. Shallower simulation keeps the project within clear research boundaries but allows skeptics to dismiss it as unrepresentative. None of the options adequately address how to handle the inevitable gap between demonstrator success in a controlled environment and the messiness of real procurement and institutional integration.

levers.name: Incumbent Operator Engagement Stance and Cooperative Friction
levers.consequences: The project must decide whether to seek formal buy-in from the MitID operator (inviting cooperation but risking co-option or capability suppression), maintain cordial but independent research distance (preserving autonomy but limiting institutional leverage), or pursue transparent opposition framing (clarifying stakes but burning bridges for future negotiation). Each stance affects phase three's ability to influence AltID procurement.
options: Engage the MitID operator as a formal partner in phase one through structured design review and security consultation, positioning the fallback work as complementary continuity planning rather than replacement, accepting that operator concerns may constrain demonstrator scope in exchange for institutional credibility.

Conduct independent technical research with occasional briefing to relevant stakeholders but without seeking formal approval or partnership, maintaining intellectual autonomy and avoiding co-option while risking operator resistance in phase three procurement discussions.

Frame the project explicitly as resilience work required because current MitID architecture does not meet sovereign continuity-of-service standards, engage the operator through public policy channels and AltID working groups rather than bilateral coordination, and accept that the operator may view the project as competitive pressure.
levers.review_lever: Operator engagement reflects a deeper tension: the operator is both a potential ally (they want to prove MitID is robust) and the primary institutional obstacle to change (they have no direct incentive to reduce reliance on Apple and Google). Cooperative engagement risks being used to delay or neuter platform-neutrality requirements; independent research preserves autonomy but leaves the project without institutional allies in procurement. None of the options credibly address how the project maintains influence if the operator actively opposes platform-neutrality language in AltID procurement decisions.

levers.name: Procurement Language Specificity and Flexibility Window
levers.consequences: Phase three must determine whether to demand explicit platform-neutrality, fallback-capability, and app-store independence requirements in AltID procurement, or to propose more flexible language (resilience, vendor lock-in mitigation, continuity planning) that is harder for stakeholders to reject but easier to hollow out in implementation. Overly prescriptive language invites legal challenge and incumbent pushback; vague language avoids rejection but may vanish in technical interpretation.
options: Propose mandatory procurement language stating that Danish digital identity systems shall not depend exclusively on any single mobile operating system, app-store distribution channel, or proprietary trust API, with explicit architectural review criteria and signed supplier commitments, accepting the risk that this will be legally contested or watered down in negotiation.

Draft resilience and contingency requirements framed as business-continuity obligations (all critical authentication paths must have certified fallback mechanisms independent of any single vendor), using existing procurement and risk-management frameworks to embed platform-neutrality without naming Apple or Google directly.

Co-author AltID procurement language with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen that includes optional capability tracks for platform-neutral and fallback authentication, creating a preference gradient rather than a hard mandate, so that vendors can offer platform-neutral features as a premium differentiator without being forced to exclude proprietary app-based access.
levers.review_lever: Language specificity determines whether the project's evidence is translated into binding procurement reality or remains advisory. Highly specific language is more defensible legally but easier to oppose and more vulnerable to challenge by incumbent vendors or platform providers with economic interest. Flexible language using existing risk and resilience frameworks survives longer in institutional process but may be reinterpreted away from platform-neutrality intent during detailed implementation. None of the options address the gap between what procurement language says and what vendors actually deliver when certification, testing, and go-live timelines arrive.

levers.name: EU Standards Alignment versus Danish Sovereign Assertion
levers.consequences: Phase three includes engagement with EU-level European Digital Identity Wallet and eIDAS2 working groups. The project must decide whether to position platform-neutrality and fallback authentication as European requirements that Denmark must implement (stronger institutional lever but depends on EU consensus), or as Danish sovereign contingency planning that merely seeks alignment with emerging EU best practice (weaker pressure but avoids sovereignty friction and does not require unanimous EU backing).
options: Lead or co-sponsor formal input to the European Digital Identity Wallet technical standards group proposing that certified fallback authentication and vendor-lock-in mitigation be included in the baseline eIDAS2 framework, leveraging the project's demonstrators as evidence that such requirements are technically feasible, and framing it as a collective EU digital sovereignty issue.

Position the project as Danish implementation of EU resilience and interoperability principles already implicit in eIDAS2 (portability, interoperability, reduced single-vendor dependency), using EU-level language to justify Danish procurement requirements without proposing new EU rules, and accepting that this is a narrower argument with less pressure on other member states.

Pursue primarily Danish-focused procurement and regulatory change while maintaining light engagement with EU bodies for visibility and alignment purposes, avoiding the complexity of EU standards negotiation and accepting that Danish influence is limited to AltID and related domestic systems rather than broader European framework.
levers.review_lever: EU engagement multiplies leverage (if eIDAS2 includes platform-neutrality language, Denmark can cite regulatory compliance) but requires consensus and slow institutional timelines. Danish-focused assertion is faster but leaves the project isolated if other member states or the EC prioritize vendor integration over contingency planning. The core tension is that genuine EU standards change takes 18–36 months and requires coalition-building across diverse national interests; the project's 24-month window may be too short to secure EU-level language that actually affects Danish procurement by month 24.

levers.name: Demonstrator Publication Timing and Pre-Procurement Visibility
levers.consequences: Releasing the working mobile and fallback demonstrators (code, architecture, security review findings) before, during, or after phase three's AltID procurement engagement affects how stakeholders perceive the evidence. Early release (month 14–15, before formal procurement drafting) builds momentum and signals mature readiness; delayed release (month 20+, after procurement decisions are made) risks becoming an afterthought but allows time for deeper security hardening and reduces the chance that vendors cite early-stage findings to dismiss the entire category.
options: Publish the demonstrators and full technical report by month 15, before AltID procurement language is finalized, creating maximum leverage for procurement negotiation while the evidence is fresh, accepting the risk that early-stage demonstrators may be criticized for incomplete security review or missing enterprise features.

Sequence publication to align with specific AltID decision gates (e.g., publish the mobile demonstrator when AltID vendor selection occurs, fallback demonstrator when detailed technical requirements are drafted), using staged releases to maintain attention and evidence relevance throughout phase three without overwhelming a single moment.

Hold all demonstrator code, architecture findings, and external validation until the end of month 24 or shortly after, publishing a comprehensive final report with complete security audit and lessons-learned, accepting that procurement decisions may already be locked in but ensuring the project's evidence is maximally defensible and complete.
levers.review_lever: Publication timing is a lever on institutional attention and evidence impact. Earlier publication builds pressure and visibility but leaves demonstrators vulnerable to criticism for immaturity; later publication ensures higher quality but risks arriving after procurement decisions are already made by procurement teams with momentum and vendor commitments. The core tension is that AltID's actual procurement timeline is not fully visible to the project, so there is genuine uncertainty about when critical decision gates occur. None of the options address the risk that early publication becomes a lightning rod for incumbent vendor criticism, while late publication may be ignored because the conversation has already moved on.

levers.name: Coalition Scope and Veto-Player Representation
levers.consequences: Phase one builds a coalition including civil-society privacy advocates, academic security researchers, contingency-planning stakeholders, and sympathetic Folketinget members. The project must decide whether to include potential veto players (e.g., banking-sector representatives, insurance industry risk officers, public-sector procurement officials, or representatives of existing digital identity users) in the coalition or to build a coalition of advocates and keep veto players at arm's length for engagement only when specific decisions arise.
options: Actively recruit banking-sector security officers, insurance-industry contingency-planning leads, and public-sector procurement specialists as formal coalition members from month 2 onward, using their risk-management authority to frame platform-neutrality as an enterprise resilience requirement rather than an advocacy cause, accepting that their participation may moderate messaging and slow coalition consensus.

Build a core advocacy coalition of researchers, privacy advocates, and sympathetic politicians, then conduct structured engagements with veto players (banks, insurance, procurement) through formal briefings, policy consultations, and design-review sessions without making them members, preserving coalition autonomy while creating feedback loops from institutional stakeholders.

Maintain separate coalitions: an advocacy track focused on civil society, academia, and policy advocacy, and a parallel stakeholder engagement track focused on bilateral relationships with financial institutions, critical infrastructure operators, and procurement authorities, using different messaging and governance for each, recognizing that formal coalition membership may not be feasible or desirable across such diverse interests.
levers.review_lever: Coalition membership determines both political reach and message coherence. Inclusive coalitions with veto players (banks, insurance, procurement) carry greater institutional weight and are harder to dismiss, but they dilute the autonomy of advocacy and may require watering down platform-neutrality claims to accommodate business-continuity and cost concerns. Narrower coalitions of advocates remain unified and clear but may lack the institutional authority to convince procurement officers or vendor-selection committees. The core tension is that actors with real procurement power (procurement officials, bank security officers) may not be willing to join a coalition explicitly framed around reducing supplier concentration, but without their participation, the coalition's phase three influence on actual procurement language is limited to advisory input.

levers.name: Contingency De-Scoping and Phase-Three Pivot Criteria
levers.consequences: If formal AltID procurement engagement yields weak results by month 16, the project must decide whether to continue pushing on procurement (accepting lower probability of success but maintaining the original goal), pivot toward EU standards work and coalition consolidation (accepting that Danish impact may be limited), or combine both with reduced resources. Clear de-scoping criteria set in advance prevent mission creep and allow realistic contingency planning; vague criteria create uncertainty and risk burning budget on diminishing returns.
options: Establish explicit go/no-go criteria at month 16: if Digitaliseringsstyrelsen has not incorporated platform-neutrality language into draft AltID requirements and the MitID operator has not committed to fallback-authentication design review, de-scope phase three toward publication, EU standards input, and coalition documentation, treating Danish procurement influence as a failed lever and redirecting effort to longer-term EU-level work.

Plan phase three with built-in flexibility: maintain both a primary track (AltID procurement influence) and a secondary track (EU standards and coalition consolidation), allocate budget dynamically based on progress signals at month 16, scaling down procurement work if institutional traction is weak while ramping up EU engagement and publication to maximize the value of work already completed.

Commit fully to procurement-focused phase three regardless of month-16 signals, extending timeline if necessary and accepting that success may arrive later than month 24, recognizing that procurement timelines in Danish government are often slower than project plans assume and that persistent engagement sometimes yields results after initial setbacks.
levers.review_lever: De-scoping criteria reduce the risk of throwing good effort after bad, but they also create a bias toward abandoning a lever too early if initial feedback is discouraging. Conversely, refusing to de-scope locks the project into a failing strategy and wastes budget. The core tension is that procurement influence is inherently uncertain and path-dependent — a single champion or decision-gate shift in month 18 can suddenly open institutional doors that seemed sealed at month 16. None of the options credibly address how to distinguish between 'the lever is failing and should be abandoned' and 'the lever is maturing and will yield results in another two months if we persist'.

levers.name: Coalition Composition and Advocacy Reach
levers.consequences: The breadth and credibility of the coalition—spanning civil society, academic researchers, security experts, contingency planners, and parliamentary allies—determines whether platform-neutrality framing gains traction as a resilience issue or remains marginal technical criticism. A coalition that includes privacy advocates and procurement-transparency organizations can reframe the dependency as a public-sector risk rather than ideology, but coalition-building during phase one competes for limited engagement capacity and may absorb political capital that later procurement work requires.
options: Recruit a cross-sector coalition (NGOs, academia, security researchers, parliamentary members) early in phase one, with explicit governance and public-facing communications to amplify resilience framing before institutional actors entrench positions.

Prioritize deep relationships with two or three anchor institutions (one government agency, one university, one civil-society organization) and let organic network growth follow, accepting slower reach but reducing coordination overhead and dependency on volunteer engagement.

Build a coalition primarily through a formally constituted steering committee of government procurement and digital-identity specialists, reducing external stakeholder involvement but increasing institutional legitimacy and direct procurement influence over time.
levers.review: Early coalition visibility risks signaling intent to incumbent platform vendors and MitID operators, potentially triggering institutional defensiveness or delays in phase-two technical partnerships. The options assume coalition members will sustain engagement across 24 months without explicit incentive structures or decision-making power, but parliamentary and NGO stakeholders may deprioritize the issue if competing digital-policy agendas emerge.

levers.name: Demonstrator Technical Scope and Standards Choice
levers.consequences: The demonstrator choice between FIDO2/WebAuthn plus OpenID Connect on GrapheneOS versus a browser-based or hardware-token fallback path determines both technical credibility and institutional acceptance. FIDO2 has broader standards recognition but remains tied to mobile OS ecosystems; hardware tokens offer genuine platform independence but introduce usability and deployment friction that procurement stakeholders may view as impractical for public-sector citizen access.
options: Develop the primary demonstrator using FIDO2/WebAuthn on GrapheneOS with a secondary hardware-token fallback, positioning mobile-first access as the main pathway and hardware tokens as a continuity reserve for critical scenarios where platform policy changes block app access.

Reverse priority and develop the hardware-token demonstrator first, using it as proof that citizen authentication can function entirely outside the Apple/Google ecosystem, then add a secondary mobile demonstrator as an ergonomic enhancement rather than the core trust path.

Build two equally resourced demonstrators in parallel—one FIDO2/WebAuthn mobile path and one browser-based fallback using server-side authentication and session persistence—to avoid committing to a single standards approach and to test institutional appetite for each before phase three procurement work begins.
levers.review: Demonstrators must convince procurement teams that platform-neutral access does not impose unacceptable usability costs on citizens, yet all three options assume that a non-production proof-of-concept will sufficiently lower institutional risk perception. None of the options account for the likelihood that procurers will demand production hardening, liability frameworks, and carrier or banking-sector certification before considering alternatives to proven MitID infrastructure.

levers.name: Regulatory Pathway and eIDAS2 Alignment
levers.consequences: The project's ability to shape AltID and future procurement depends on establishing clear eIDAS2 Substantial-level compliance and NSIS conformance for platform-neutral alternatives before procurement specifications freeze. Early regulatory clarity increases institutional confidence but narrows design space; delayed regulatory engagement allows technical flexibility but risks arriving at phase three with no clear path to formal compliance, leaving procurers with no grounds to require platform-neutral alternatives.
options: Engage Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and EU Digital Identity Regulation working groups in phase one, establishing formal regulatory positions and gaining early feedback on eIDAS2 conformance barriers, accepting slower technical iteration but building institutional confidence that platform-neutral designs can meet certification requirements.

Defer regulatory engagement until phase two is complete, allowing demonstrators to inform realistic compliance requirements and risk assessment, then use evidence from working prototypes to negotiate regulatory positions with government bodies in phase three with greater technical certainty.

Pursue parallel engagement with both Danish national authorities and European Commission technical working groups from phase one onward, positioning the project as an EU-level standards contribution rather than a national procurement intervention, which broadens influence but dilutes focus on Danish AltID outcomes.
levers.review: eIDAS2 certification timelines and approval gates are outside the project's control, and regulatory bodies may not formally respond to platform-neutrality requirements until broader European Digital Identity Wallet rules solidify. The options assume that regulatory engagement will yield cooperative movement toward platform-neutral certification requirements, but incumbent operators and conservative certification bodies may argue that splitting the trust model between platform-dependent and platform-neutral paths introduces unmanageable assurance complexity.

levers.name: Incumbent Operator Engagement and Non-Cooperation Risk
levers.consequences: MitID's operator and Apple/Google relationships represent structural barriers to phase-two demonstrator credibility and phase-three procurement leverage. Constructive engagement with MitID operators could provide technical clarification and institutional legitimacy, but risks becoming co-opted into incremental improvements that do not address platform dependency, or facing deliberate obstruction if operators perceive the project as a threat to their commercial position.
options: Seek formal technical partnership with the MitID operator in phase one, framing the demonstrator work as a continuity-planning exercise and fallback capability that complements rather than competes with MitID, accepting risk of co-optation but gaining access to technical specifications and institutional credibility.

Treat MitID operators as a stakeholder constituency to be informed rather than engaged, keeping demonstrator work technically independent and transparent, publishing findings openly to create institutional pressure for platform-neutral design without seeking operator approval or partnership.

Establish a formal non-cooperation framework from phase one, with clear technical and communication boundaries, acknowledging that operators may not cooperate constructively and structuring work to avoid dependence on operator consent or technical disclosure, at the cost of reduced institutional legitimacy with some government bodies.
levers.review: MitID operators have strong incentives to maintain current platform dependencies and may interpret demonstrator work as an implicit criticism of existing infrastructure. The options assume the project can succeed with operator non-cooperation or lukewarm cooperation, but if procurers view the MitID operator's assessment of fallback-authentication risks as authoritative, the project's recommendations may be dismissed as technically unsound or operationally unfeasible regardless of demonstrator evidence.

levers.name: Procurement Intervention Timing and AltID Engagement Window
levers.consequences: AltID rollout timeline and procurement specification windows narrow as planning moves from phase one (months 1–8) into phase two and three. Early procurement engagement increases influence but risks overcommitting before demonstrators and feasibility reports are ready; late engagement preserves technical credibility but may arrive after critical architecture decisions have been locked in by institutional inertia or vendor planning cycles.
options: Initiate formal procurement engagement in parallel with phase-one research output (month 6–8), presenting the feasibility report and coalition position to AltID governance as input to ongoing design consultation, accepting risk of incomplete technical evidence but capturing the planning window before specifications freeze.

Delay major procurement outreach until phase-two demonstrators are complete (month 14–16), using working prototypes and documented standards compliance to support platform-neutrality requirements during AltID procurement drafting, trading earlier influence for stronger technical leverage.

Maintain continuous low-level procurement engagement throughout phases one and two (quarterly briefings, working-group participation) rather than concentrated pushes, distributing influence across the planning cycle but diffusing institutional attention and requiring sustained coalition energy.
levers.review: AltID procurement timelines are set by government digital transformation roadmaps, not by this project, and institutional planning horizons may already be committed to platform-dependent architecture. The options assume that procurement teams will be receptive to fallback-authentication requirements once technical feasibility is demonstrated, but if competing priorities (cost control, rapid deployment, incumbent vendor relationships) dominate procurement decisions, the project's evidence will have minimal effect on final specifications.

levers.name: EU Standards Contribution versus Danish-Focused Outcome
levers.consequences: Phase-three EU engagement through European Digital Identity Wallet working groups and standards bodies can amplify platform-neutrality language at a regulatory level, potentially influencing broader eIDAS2 implementation across member states and creating downstream pressure on Danish procurement. However, EU-level advocacy dilutes focus on Danish AltID outcomes and introduces dependency on other member states' priorities, timelines, and political will; the project may gain EU-level influence while failing to secure platform-neutrality requirements in actual Danish procurement.
options: Prioritize EU-level engagement as a phase-three strategy, framing platform-neutrality as a member-state resilience and digital-sovereignty issue within broader European Digital Identity Wallet discussions, with the goal of securing EU-level language that later constrains or encourages Danish procurement decisions.

Keep EU engagement subordinate to Danish procurement focus, using European working groups as a forum for validating technical work and positioning Denmark as a resilience leader, but concentrating primary effort on securing platform-neutrality requirements in AltID rather than pursuing European regulatory influence.

Pursue parallel Danish and EU tracks from phase one, allocating resources to both procurement intervention and standards contribution, accepting diluted focus but positioning the project as a bridge between national digital sovereignty concerns and European digital-identity harmonization.
levers.review: European Digital Identity Wallet standards and member-state adoption timelines are not synchronized with Danish AltID deployment, and platform-neutrality language at the EU level does not automatically translate into binding procurement requirements for member states. The options assume that EU-level engagement will eventually reinforce Danish procurement decisions, but EU standards bodies may prioritize interoperability and wallet functionality over supplier concentration concerns, leaving platform-neutrality language as aspirational rather than mandatory.

levers.name: Success Measurement and Phase-Three De-Scoping Conditions
levers.consequences: The project defines success through institutional outcomes (formal AltID inclusion of platform-neutrality language) and published evidence, not through consumer adoption or production deployment. If phase-three procurement engagement yields weak institutional traction by month 16, the project plan anticipates de-scoping toward publication and coalition consolidation rather than forcing procurement influence. This disciplined exit prevents resource waste but risks communicating that platform-neutrality requirements are optional and creates perception of failure if AltID does not formally adopt the project's recommendations.
options: Establish a hard go/no-go decision point at month 16, assessing whether procurement stakeholders have demonstrated genuine engagement with platform-neutrality proposals; if engagement is weak, de-scope phase three toward publication and EU standards work, accepting that AltID may not be directly influenced but preserving project credibility and evidence contribution.

Front-load success criteria into phases one and two (feasibility report publication, demonstrator delivery, formal institutional response), treating phase-three procurement outcomes as contingent on external conditions beyond the project's control, reducing expectation that procurement adoption is guaranteed and focusing instead on deliverable evidence.

Expand phase-three success criteria beyond AltID to include secondary institutional influences (banking-sector adoption of fallback authentication, regional government procurement alignment, parliamentary digital-policy committee endorsement), broadening the definition of success to reduce dependence on a single procurement outcome.
levers.review: AltID procurement decisions are driven by government digital transformation budgets, vendor relationships, and deployment timelines that predate this project and may be largely locked in by phase three. The options assume that institutional decision-makers will be persuaded by feasibility reports and demonstrators to revise procurement specifications toward platform-neutrality, but procurement momentum and incumbent vendor contracts may make significant architecture changes politically and budgetarily infeasible regardless of technical evidence presented.

levers.name: Demonstrator Credibility Architecture and Integration Depth
levers.consequences: Choosing how deeply the demonstrators integrate with real Danish authentication infrastructure (simulated MitID environment, live Digitaliseringsstyrelsen test systems, or purely standalone) determines both technical credibility and political safety. Deeper integration (test-system access) produces stronger proof-of-concept but risks operational friction with incumbent operators or demands early regulatory approval that may stall the project. Standalone demonstrators preserve control and speed but invite dismissal as academically interesting but operationally detached.
options: Build demonstrators against fully simulated Danish public-sector identity-provider infrastructure without requesting live test-system access, ensuring technical autonomy and avoiding early escalation to MitID or Digitaliseringsstyrelsen gatekeepers.

Negotiate limited read-only or sandboxed access to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen's eIDAS2 test environment by month 12, framing the request as validation of inter-standard compatibility rather than as an implicit threat to MitID.

Partner with a sympathetic regional municipality or public agency to conduct a small-scale pilot authentication workflow using the demonstrator against real public-sector backend systems, creating operational legitimacy while maintaining distance from national identity operators.
levers.review: Deeper integration with real systems strengthens the project's legitimacy as a governance and procurement intervention, but none of the options account for the timeline risk: regulatory approvals for test-system access can stretch 4–8 months, compressing phase-two technical delivery. The fallback option (municipal pilot) avoids central gatekeepers but creates a new dependency on local political appetite and may expose the project to early incumbent pushback if MitID operators learn of parallel authentication testing in the public sector.

levers.name: Standards Contribution versus Standards Avoidance
levers.consequences: Engaging actively with EU technical working groups (ETSI, eIDAS2 revision bodies) on platform neutrality language enhances the project's influence on European Digital Identity Wallet specifications and creates political cover for Danish procurement intervention. Active engagement also accelerates learning on emerging interoperability standards and potential regulatory tailwinds. Conversely, high-profile standards involvement raises the project's visibility to incumbent operators earlier and may invite competitive or defensive responses from platform vendors and their allies in telecom and banking sectors.
options: Contribute formal position papers and technical input to ETSI and eIDAS2 working groups starting in phase two, positioning the project as an independent voice advocating for platform-neutral and resilience-oriented language in emerging European standards.

Engage with EU bodies only after phase two demonstrators are complete and polished, maximizing technical credibility before entering high-stakes standards discussions and reducing the risk of premature incumbent opposition.

Defer direct standards engagement and instead publish peer-reviewed academic papers on platform-neutral Danish identity architecture in open journals and conference proceedings, allowing the project to influence standards conversations through scholarly authority rather than institutional advocacy.
levers.review: Early standards engagement amplifies the project's leverage but creates visibility risk: incumbent operators actively monitor eIDAS2 and European Digital Identity Wallet working groups, and early disclosure of platform-neutrality arguments may trigger organized counter-arguments or regulatory pressure. The academic-publications strategy delays visibility but also delays influence—by the time papers are published and cited in standards discussions, AltID procurement may already be locked into Apple and Google dependencies, making the European standards window less relevant to Danish implementation.

levers.name: Coalition Widening and Veto-Player Management
levers.consequences: Expanding the coalition beyond civil-society and academic stakeholders to include sympathetic voices within Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the banking sector, or the telecom industry magnifies institutional influence but risks compromising the project's framing if commercial partners demand messaging that dilutes the sovereignty and resilience narrative. Narrower coalitions preserve clarity and independence but reduce access to decision-makers and implementation capacity.
options: Build a coalition exclusively with civil-society organisations, privacy advocates, academic researchers, and security experts, maintaining editorial control over messaging and avoiding entanglement with commercial interests that profit from the current MitID ecosystem.

Actively recruit banking and fintech leaders who face operational constraints under Apple/Google payment-system policies, positioning platform neutrality as a commercial-resilience issue that benefits the private sector alongside public digital sovereignty.

Identify and cultivate relationships with procurement officers, IT security leads, and business-continuity planners within Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and major public-sector agencies, framing platform neutrality as an operational and contingency-planning imperative independent of policy ideology.
levers.review: Including commercial partners (banking, fintech) broadens the political base for procurement intervention and adds credibility on operational constraints, but none of the options anticipate the conflict-of-interest dynamics: banks benefit from Apple/Google dominance for payment-system lock-in, and fintech firms often depend on platform app stores for distribution. A coalition that includes both digital-sovereignty advocates and commercial partners risks internal credibility loss if these actors' interests diverge during the critical AltID procurement negotiation window.

levers.name: Fallback Mechanism Design Philosophy and Trust Model
levers.consequences: Choosing the fallback authentication path—browser-based, hardware-token, or a hybrid approach reducing but not eliminating mobile OS dependence—shapes both technical feasibility and the strength of the governance argument. Browser-based fallback avoids OS lock-in but requires infrastructure (government-hosted authentication endpoints) that may face resistance from MitID operators. Hardware tokens are resilient but introduce cost and UX barriers to citizen adoption. The choice also determines whether the fallback is truly platform-neutral or merely shifts dependence to a different vendor ecosystem.
options: Design the fallback as a browser-based OpenID Connect workflow integrated with a government-operated digital identity provider, eliminating dependence on mobile app stores and OS-level integrity signals but requiring new public-sector infrastructure and security certification.

Develop a hardware-token fallback using open standards such as FIDO2 and USB or NFC interfaces, providing strong platform neutrality and resilience but introducing cost, logistics, and citizen-literacy barriers that may limit practical adoption in a national-scale identity system.

Create a hybrid fallback combining a privacy-focused Android alternative (GrapheneOS) with WebAuthn for authentication, accepting residual OS-level dependence on the Android ecosystem while reducing the degree of platform vendor lock-in and avoiding government infrastructure expansion.
levers.review: Browser-based fallback provides the cleanest platform neutrality but requires government to operate a new authentication infrastructure with its own security and liability implications, creating an institutional burden that Digitaliseringsstyrelsen may resist. Hardware-token fallback maximizes resilience but the usability and distribution costs create a political opening for incumbent operators to argue that platform neutrality sacrifices citizen convenience. The hybrid Android-based approach sidesteps both infrastructure and usability problems but weakens the sovereignty argument—shifting from Apple/Google lock-in to Android/Google lock-in is a partial solution that may not satisfy the political motivation for reduced foreign vendor dependence.

levers.name: AltID Engagement Sequencing and Pressure Application
levers.consequences: Timing intervention in the AltID design and procurement process determines whether the project can influence requirements before institutional momentum hardens. Early engagement (months 9–12) allows input into initial design discussions but risks appearing premature if demonstrators are incomplete. Late engagement (months 18–20) ensures technical credibility but collides with final procurement decisions when flexibility is minimal. The choice also determines whether the project frames itself as a collaborative design partner or as an external pressure and standards advocate.
options: Request formal technical review slots in the official AltID design working groups by month 10, presenting demonstrator prototypes and feasibility findings as design inputs and framing the project as constructive technical contributor to the AltID roadmap.

Withhold direct AltID engagement until month 16–18 when demonstrators are complete and polished, then deliver a comprehensive technical and policy report to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and publicly, using peer-reviewed credibility and external validation to create procurement pressure at the critical decision gate.

Maintain informal advisory relationships with sympathetic AltID stakeholders throughout the project, gathering intelligence on design decisions and procurement timelines while building the coalition, then deploy concentrated advocacy and technical evidence at inflection points when procurement language is actually being drafted.
levers.review: Early collaborative engagement offers influence but depends on AltID leadership's openness to platform-neutrality messaging—if institutional positions are already set, early requests for design review are routed to technical committees and deprioritized. Late high-profile intervention maximizes technical credibility but may arrive after procurement language is drafted, limiting ability to shape requirements. The informal advisory approach preserves flexibility and coalition-building time but creates information asymmetry: the project may not detect procurement milestones until they have already passed or are committed in non-public stakeholder agreements.

levers.name: Regulatory Pathway Clarity and eIDAS2 Compliance Framing
levers.consequences: Explicitly stating that platform-neutral and fallback-capable systems can meet eIDAS2 Substantial-level assurance requirements reframes the issue from 'regulatory burden' to 'regulatory permission,' lowering political resistance. Producing detailed compliance evidence (gap analyses, threat models, test procedures) makes it harder for incumbent operators to argue that platform neutrality creates unacceptable assurance gaps. However, this reframing also invites detailed regulatory scrutiny and may trigger formal consultations with supervisory bodies, extending timelines and introducing new veto points.
options: Commission an independent legal and technical analysis of eIDAS2 and NSIS Substantial-level assurance requirements, documenting specific regulatory pathways through which platform-neutral and fallback authentication architectures can demonstrate equivalent or superior security posture compared to current Apple/Google-mediated trust models.

Work with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen's compliance and standards teams to informally discuss eIDAS2 interpretations relevant to platform neutrality, building internal regulatory confidence without triggering formal consultation processes or creating paper trails that incumbent operators can contest.

Publish technical threat models and security analyses demonstrating that platform-neutral fallback mechanisms reduce certain systemic risks (single points of failure, geopolitical vendor lock-in, app-store distribution vulnerabilities) relative to the current architecture, reframing the compliance conversation around resilience rather than equivalence.
levers.review: Formal independent compliance analysis is the most credible approach but risks regulatory capture: if the analyst is perceived as aligned with the project's advocacy goals, incumbent operators will challenge the findings as biased. Informal regulatory discussions with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen preserve speed and flexibility but create no formal record or institutional commitment, allowing regulators to deny or reinterpret earlier conversations when procurement disputes arise. Published threat models shift the conversation away from equivalence—a favorable move—but without formal eIDAS2 guidance, threat-model framing can be dismissed as theoretical rather than operationally binding.
