levers.name: Phase-Three Contingency and De-Scoping Conditions
levers.consequences: Explicitly defining the conditions under which phase three pivots from AltID procurement intervention toward EU standards and coalition consolidation preserves project credibility and prevents sunk-cost escalation. If formal AltID traction is weak by month 16, shifting to publication and EU-facing standards work is politically honest but reduces immediate Danish procurement impact. Conversely, committing to AltID intervention regardless of institutional receptiveness risks phase-three budget burn and project-credibility damage if the final outcome is symbolic language without procurement teeth.
options: Establish clear phase-three go/no-go criteria by month 15: if Digitaliseringsstyrelsen has not formally incorporated platform-neutrality or fallback-access language into official AltID design documents or procurement briefs, the project automatically de-scopes toward academic publication, EU standards contribution, and coalition archive; reallocate remaining phase-three budget (approximately 1.5 million DKK) toward long-term advocacy infrastructure and follow-on funding applications.

Negotiate explicit outcome commitments from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and the AltID program leadership by month 12, formalizing written agreements on how demonstrator findings and policy recommendations will feed into procurement requirements; if commitments are not honored by month 18, trigger a public-stakeholder review and media engagement strategy to escalate pressure before final AltID decisions.

Preserve phase-three optionality by maintaining active AltID engagement and coalition-building throughout the project without pre-committing to institutional success criteria, allowing the project leadership to make month-16 decisions based on actual political conditions and demonstrator reception rather than pre-set conditions.
levers.review: Clear go/no-go criteria provide disciplined risk management and preserve credibility, but the automatic de-scope option risks appearing as project failure to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and future funders, even if EU standards and coalition outcomes are substantial. Negotiated outcome commitments create formal accountability and prevent mission creep, but Digitaliseringsstyrelsen will resist binding language that constrains their AltID flexibility. Preserving optionality allows adaptive decision-making at month 16 but defers the hardest institutional and financial decisions to a point when demonstrators are complete and political positions have hardened, potentially trapping the project between competing advocacy strategies with insufficient budget to execute all of them credibly.

levers.name: Trust Anchor Migration and Fallback Authentication Binding
levers.consequences: Shifting which entity (MitID operator, public identity provider, or a hybrid model) certifies and manages the fallback authentication path determines both technical feasibility and institutional acceptance. If MitID retains control, the fallback remains within the existing trust envelope but may preserve dependencies; if a public identity provider assumes certification, it creates a parallel trust path but requires new institutional coordination and legal frameworks.
options: Propose that the Danish public identity provider (not MitID operator) takes responsibility for certifying a platform-neutral fallback path, accepting institutional friction but creating a genuinely independent authentication route separate from the app-ecosystem mediation.

Design a hybrid trust model where MitID retains primary certification for mainstream access but explicitly delegates fallback or continuity authentication to a separately governed, standards-aligned identity service that uses open protocols.

Architect a distributed trust model using cryptographic proof-of-work or threshold signatures to reduce reliance on any single institutional certifier, transferring the trust anchor from vendor or operator to distributed ledger infrastructure or multi-party computation.
levers.review: Shifting trust anchors solves platform dependency on paper, but the core tension is institutional: whoever certifies the fallback path must be trusted by the entire ecosystem (banks, tax authority, healthcare systems) and must be perceived as neutral and competent. None of the options address how to bootstrap trust in a new certifying body that has no track record managing production authentication for critical services. Additionally, if the fallback path is rarely used, institutional will to maintain it atrophies over time.

levers.name: Demonstrator Integration Depth and Production Proximity
levers.consequences: Deciding how closely the phase-two demonstrators mirror real MitID architecture and live public-sector identity providers affects credibility and risk exposure. Deeper integration (test signing with real identity-provider certificates, integration with actual banking sandboxes) generates stronger proof-of-concept but increases regulatory oversight and incident liability; shallow integration (purely simulated environments, synthetic data) remains defensible but is more easily dismissed as academic.
options: Build demonstrators using non-production sandbox credentials issued specifically by Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or MitID operator for this project, integrating with real identity-provider test systems so that the proof-of-concept is validated against live institutional infrastructure without touching production authentication.

Construct entirely simulated identity-provider environments using open-standards reference implementations, ensuring the demonstrators are fully self-contained and cannot be mistaken for production systems, prioritizing legal and regulatory clarity over integration depth.

Develop a staged demonstrator pathway where the mobile and fallback authenticators begin in simulation but include architectural hooks and documented migration paths that could allow incremental integration with real public-sector systems after phase two, signaling scalability without immediate production coupling.
levers.review: Integration depth trades off credibility against complexity and liability. Deeper integration strengthens the message that platform-neutral authentication is technically viable, but it also raises institutional concerns about whether the project is attempting a stealth product deployment. Shallower simulation keeps the project within clear research boundaries but allows skeptics to dismiss it as unrepresentative. None of the options adequately address how to handle the inevitable gap between demonstrator success in a controlled environment and the messiness of real procurement and institutional integration.

levers.name: Incumbent Operator Engagement Stance and Cooperative Friction
levers.consequences: The project must decide whether to seek formal buy-in from the MitID operator (inviting cooperation but risking co-option or capability suppression), maintain cordial but independent research distance (preserving autonomy but limiting institutional leverage), or pursue transparent opposition framing (clarifying stakes but burning bridges for future negotiation). Each stance affects phase three's ability to influence AltID procurement.
options: Engage the MitID operator as a formal partner in phase one through structured design review and security consultation, positioning the fallback work as complementary continuity planning rather than replacement, accepting that operator concerns may constrain demonstrator scope in exchange for institutional credibility.

Conduct independent technical research with occasional briefing to relevant stakeholders but without seeking formal approval or partnership, maintaining intellectual autonomy and avoiding co-option while risking operator resistance in phase three procurement discussions.

Frame the project explicitly as resilience work required because current MitID architecture does not meet sovereign continuity-of-service standards, engage the operator through public policy channels and AltID working groups rather than bilateral coordination, and accept that the operator may view the project as competitive pressure.
levers.review: Operator engagement reflects a deeper tension: the operator is both a potential ally (they want to prove MitID is robust) and the primary institutional obstacle to change (they have no direct incentive to reduce reliance on Apple and Google). Cooperative engagement risks being used to delay or neuter platform-neutrality requirements; independent research preserves autonomy but leaves the project without institutional allies in procurement. None of the options credibly address how the project maintains influence if the operator actively opposes platform-neutrality language in AltID procurement decisions.

levers.name: Procurement Language Specificity and Flexibility Window
levers.consequences: Phase three must determine whether to demand explicit platform-neutrality, fallback-capability, and app-store independence requirements in AltID procurement, or to propose more flexible language (resilience, vendor lock-in mitigation, continuity planning) that is harder for stakeholders to reject but easier to hollow out in implementation. Overly prescriptive language invites legal challenge and incumbent pushback; vague language avoids rejection but may vanish in technical interpretation.
options: Propose mandatory procurement language stating that Danish digital identity systems shall not depend exclusively on any single mobile operating system, app-store distribution channel, or proprietary trust API, with explicit architectural review criteria and signed supplier commitments, accepting the risk that this will be legally contested or watered down in negotiation.

Draft resilience and contingency requirements framed as business-continuity obligations (all critical authentication paths must have certified fallback mechanisms independent of any single vendor), using existing procurement and risk-management frameworks to embed platform-neutrality without naming Apple or Google directly.

Co-author AltID procurement language with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen that includes optional capability tracks for platform-neutral and fallback authentication, creating a preference gradient rather than a hard mandate, so that vendors can offer platform-neutral features as a premium differentiator without being forced to exclude proprietary app-based access.
levers.review: Language specificity determines whether the project's evidence is translated into binding procurement reality or remains advisory. Highly specific language is more defensible legally but easier to oppose and more vulnerable to challenge by incumbent vendors or platform providers with economic interest. Flexible language using existing risk and resilience frameworks survives longer in institutional process but may be reinterpreted away from platform-neutrality intent during detailed implementation. None of the options address the gap between what procurement language says and what vendors actually deliver when certification, testing, and go-live timelines arrive.

levers.name: EU Standards Alignment versus Danish Sovereign Assertion
levers.consequences: Phase three includes engagement with EU-level European Digital Identity Wallet and eIDAS2 working groups. The project must decide whether to position platform-neutrality and fallback authentication as European requirements that Denmark must implement (stronger institutional lever but depends on EU consensus), or as Danish sovereign contingency planning that merely seeks alignment with emerging EU best practice (weaker pressure but avoids sovereignty friction and does not require unanimous EU backing).
options: Lead or co-sponsor formal input to the European Digital Identity Wallet technical standards group proposing that certified fallback authentication and vendor-lock-in mitigation be included in the baseline eIDAS2 framework, leveraging the project's demonstrators as evidence that such requirements are technically feasible, and framing it as a collective EU digital sovereignty issue.

Position the project as Danish implementation of EU resilience and interoperability principles already implicit in eIDAS2 (portability, interoperability, reduced single-vendor dependency), using EU-level language to justify Danish procurement requirements without proposing new EU rules, and accepting that this is a narrower argument with less pressure on other member states.

Pursue primarily Danish-focused procurement and regulatory change while maintaining light engagement with EU bodies for visibility and alignment purposes, avoiding the complexity of EU standards negotiation and accepting that Danish influence is limited to AltID and related domestic systems rather than broader European framework.
levers.review: EU engagement multiplies leverage (if eIDAS2 includes platform-neutrality language, Denmark can cite regulatory compliance) but requires consensus and slow institutional timelines. Danish-focused assertion is faster but leaves the project isolated if other member states or the EC prioritize vendor integration over contingency planning. The core tension is that genuine EU standards change takes 18–36 months and requires coalition-building across diverse national interests; the project's 24-month window may be too short to secure EU-level language that actually affects Danish procurement by month 24.

levers.name: Demonstrator Publication Timing and Pre-Procurement Visibility
levers.consequences: Releasing the working mobile and fallback demonstrators (code, architecture, security review findings) before, during, or after phase three's AltID procurement engagement affects how stakeholders perceive the evidence. Early release (month 14–15, before formal procurement drafting) builds momentum and signals mature readiness; delayed release (month 20+, after procurement decisions are made) risks becoming an afterthought but allows time for deeper security hardening and reduces the chance that vendors cite early-stage findings to dismiss the entire category.
options: Publish the demonstrators and full technical report by month 15, before AltID procurement language is finalized, creating maximum leverage for procurement negotiation while the evidence is fresh, accepting the risk that early-stage demonstrators may be criticized for incomplete security review or missing enterprise features.

Sequence publication to align with specific AltID decision gates (e.g., publish the mobile demonstrator when AltID vendor selection occurs, fallback demonstrator when detailed technical requirements are drafted), using staged releases to maintain attention and evidence relevance throughout phase three without overwhelming a single moment.

Hold all demonstrator code, architecture findings, and external validation until the end of month 24 or shortly after, publishing a comprehensive final report with complete security audit and lessons-learned, accepting that procurement decisions may already be locked in but ensuring the project's evidence is maximally defensible and complete.
levers.review: Publication timing is a lever on institutional attention and evidence impact. Earlier publication builds pressure and visibility but leaves demonstrators vulnerable to criticism for immaturity; later publication ensures higher quality but risks arriving after procurement decisions are already made by procurement teams with momentum and vendor commitments. The core tension is that AltID's actual procurement timeline is not fully visible to the project, so there is genuine uncertainty about when critical decision gates occur. None of the options address the risk that early publication becomes a lightning rod for incumbent vendor criticism, while late publication may be ignored because the conversation has already moved on.

levers.name: Coalition Scope and Veto-Player Representation
levers.consequences: Phase one builds a coalition including civil-society privacy advocates, academic security researchers, contingency-planning stakeholders, and sympathetic Folketinget members. The project must decide whether to include potential veto players (e.g., banking-sector representatives, insurance industry risk officers, public-sector procurement officials, or representatives of existing digital identity users) in the coalition or to build a coalition of advocates and keep veto players at arm's length for engagement only when specific decisions arise.
options: Actively recruit banking-sector security officers, insurance-industry contingency-planning leads, and public-sector procurement specialists as formal coalition members from month 2 onward, using their risk-management authority to frame platform-neutrality as an enterprise resilience requirement rather than an advocacy cause, accepting that their participation may moderate messaging and slow coalition consensus.

Build a core advocacy coalition of researchers, privacy advocates, and sympathetic politicians, then conduct structured engagements with veto players (banks, insurance, procurement) through formal briefings, policy consultations, and design-review sessions without making them members, preserving coalition autonomy while creating feedback loops from institutional stakeholders.

Maintain separate coalitions: an advocacy track focused on civil society, academia, and policy advocacy, and a parallel stakeholder engagement track focused on bilateral relationships with financial institutions, critical infrastructure operators, and procurement authorities, using different messaging and governance for each, recognizing that formal coalition membership may not be feasible or desirable across such diverse interests.
levers.review: Coalition membership determines both political reach and message coherence. Inclusive coalitions with veto players (banks, insurance, procurement) carry greater institutional weight and are harder to dismiss, but they dilute the autonomy of advocacy and may require watering down platform-neutrality claims to accommodate business-continuity and cost concerns. Narrower coalitions of advocates remain unified and clear but may lack the institutional authority to convince procurement officers or vendor-selection committees. The core tension is that actors with real procurement power (procurement officials, bank security officers) may not be willing to join a coalition explicitly framed around reducing supplier concentration, but without their participation, the coalition's phase three influence on actual procurement language is limited to advisory input.

levers.name: Contingency De-Scoping and Phase-Three Pivot Criteria
levers.consequences: If formal AltID procurement engagement yields weak results by month 16, the project must decide whether to continue pushing on procurement (accepting lower probability of success but maintaining the original goal), pivot toward EU standards work and coalition consolidation (accepting that Danish impact may be limited), or combine both with reduced resources. Clear de-scoping criteria set in advance prevent mission creep and allow realistic contingency planning; vague criteria create uncertainty and risk burning budget on diminishing returns.
options: Establish explicit go/no-go criteria at month 16: if Digitaliseringsstyrelsen has not incorporated platform-neutrality language into draft AltID requirements and the MitID operator has not committed to fallback-authentication design review, de-scope phase three toward publication, EU standards input, and coalition documentation, treating Danish procurement influence as a failed lever and redirecting effort to longer-term EU-level work.

Plan phase three with built-in flexibility: maintain both a primary track (AltID procurement influence) and a secondary track (EU standards and coalition consolidation), allocate budget dynamically based on progress signals at month 16, scaling down procurement work if institutional traction is weak while ramping up EU engagement and publication to maximize the value of work already completed.

Commit fully to procurement-focused phase three regardless of month-16 signals, extending timeline if necessary and accepting that success may arrive later than month 24, recognizing that procurement timelines in Danish government are often slower than project plans assume and that persistent engagement sometimes yields results after initial setbacks.
levers.review: De-scoping criteria reduce the risk of throwing good effort after bad, but they also create a bias toward abandoning a lever too early if initial feedback is discouraging. Conversely, refusing to de-scope locks the project into a failing strategy and wastes budget. The core tension is that procurement influence is inherently uncertain and path-dependent — a single champion or decision-gate shift in month 18 can suddenly open institutional doors that seemed sealed at month 16. None of the options credibly address how to distinguish between 'the lever is failing and should be abandoned' and 'the lever is maturing and will yield results in another two months if we persist'.

Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     A short critical review — name the core tension, then identify a weakness the options miss.
     Examples:
     - "Switching from seasonal contract labor to year-round employees stabilizes harvest quality, but none of the options price in the idle-wage burden during the 5-month off-season."
     - "Routing the light-rail extension through the historic district unlocks ridership but triggers Section 106 heritage review; the options assume permits will clear on the standard timeline."
     - "Pooling catastrophe risk across three coastal regions diversifies exposure on paper, but the options neglect that a single hurricane season can correlate all three simultaneously."
     Do not use square brackets or placeholder text.

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")

6. **Option Structure**
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach (at least 15 words with an action verb) — not a short label or vague aspiration

Plan:
Denmark's national digital identity infrastructure currently depends in practice on Apple iOS and Google Android for broad citizen access to essential services such as banking, tax filing, healthcare portals, and public administration. This dependency is not limited to app-store distribution. MitID's anti-fraud and app-integrity model relies on platform-controlled signals concerning approved installation channels and device integrity, which means that critical parts of the trust model for Danish digital identity are mediated by two foreign commercial platform providers. Even where direct observation of specific authentication events is not publicly documented, the current setup leaves Danish digital identity structurally dependent on platform vendors that control app availability, device trust signals, and surrounding telemetry environments. This creates not only a digital-sovereignty problem, but also a resilience and contingency problem: essential public access depends on a narrow supplier base and lacks a clearly platform-neutral fallback path.

This matters because Denmark's public-sector digital strategy for 2026–2029 explicitly calls for reduced dependence on a small number of large foreign technology suppliers and for stronger digital sovereignty. The issue should therefore not be framed only as privacy or ideology, but as a concrete public-sector risk involving supplier concentration, reduced strategic autonomy, continuity-of-service exposure, and the absence of a robust fallback authentication path if platform policies, integrity APIs, distribution terms, or geopolitical conditions change.

This project does not attempt to build and certify a production-ready replacement for MitID within the current trust framework. That would be unrealistic. The MitID ecosystem is conservative, certification-heavy, and tightly coupled to Apple/Google platform integrity signals. Instead, the project's objective is to establish the technical, regulatory, and political basis for formal platform-neutral access requirements and a certified fallback authentication path for Danish digital identity. The primary intervention point is not retrofitting MitID itself, but influencing AltID — Denmark's staged eIDAS2 wallet rollout — together with related procurement, standards, and continuity-planning decisions before the next identity stack hardens the same dependencies.

The project is structured in three phases over 24 months with a total budget of 10.5 million DKK, gated across phases (3.0 million for phase one, 4.0 million for phase two, 3.5 million for phase three). The higher budget reflects the need for credible technical work, policy engagement, external review, institutional overhead, and contingency capacity. Do not assume that 8 million DKK is sufficient for the same scope.

Phase one (months 1–8) is a feasibility, risk, and coalition-building phase. The technical work maps the relevant MitID dependency surface, especially platform integrity assumptions, app-distribution constraints, and the architectural conditions required for a platform-neutral or fallback trust model under eIDAS and NSIS at level "Substantial" or higher. The policy work reframes the issue in terms of resilience, continuity of service, procurement neutrality, and supplier concentration risk, while also addressing privacy and democratic accountability. In parallel, the advocacy work builds a coalition spanning civil-society organisations, privacy advocates, academic researchers, security experts, contingency-planning stakeholders, and sympathetic members of Folketinget's digital policy committee. The deliverables are: (1) a published feasibility and risk report, (2) a formal policy proposal to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, (3) a fallback-authentication concept note oriented toward AltID and future procurement requirements, and (4) a permit/approval and stakeholder matrix with lead times, decision gates, and identified blockers. Phase one funding is unconditional.

Phase two (months 9–16) develops non-production technical demonstrators. These are not certified MitID clients and do not attempt production integration with live MitID systems. The main demonstrator authenticates against a simulated Danish public-sector identity-provider environment using open standards such as FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect on GrapheneOS or one comparable privacy-respecting Android-compatible platform. A secondary demonstrator explores a browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback authentication path that reduces dependence on the mobile OS duopoly altogether. The goal is not to prove immediate deployability, but to produce credible technical evidence that secure, standards-based, fallback-capable access models are feasible without exclusive reliance on Apple or Google as trust anchors. Keep the demonstrator scope lean and defensible; do not expand into feature-rich product development. Phase two funding is conditional on phase one producing a published feasibility report, at least one formal institutional engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, or a related public-sector stakeholder, and a documented financing plan covering the remainder of the project.

Phase three (months 17–24) focuses on procurement, standards, and continuity intervention around AltID and related digital identity planning. Using the feasibility report, demonstrators, and coalition built in earlier phases, the project seeks inclusion of requirements stating that Danish digital identity systems should not depend exclusively on Apple or Google app stores, platform integrity APIs, or proprietary push-notification infrastructure, and should include a platform-neutral or otherwise certified fallback access path for critical public authentication. The project also engages with relevant EU bodies and technical working groups connected to the European Digital Identity Wallet framework, with the aim of aligning Danish advocacy with broader European requirements on interoperability, portability, resilience, and reduced platform lock-in. The project's role is not to dictate architecture directly, but to create technical evidence, procurement language, and standards pressure that make supplier-neutral and fallback-capable design harder to exclude. If formal traction with AltID is weak by month 16, phase three should de-scope toward publication, coalition consolidation, and EU-facing standards input rather than pretending Danish procurement influence has been secured.

The project team should include a lead researcher in mobile security and authentication protocols, a regulatory and compliance specialist familiar with Danish and EU digital identity frameworks (including eIDAS and NSIS), a policy and public-affairs coordinator experienced in Danish digital governance and procurement processes, a technical lead for the demonstrator work, and at least one additional engineer or contractor covering web authentication, hardware-token workflows, or Linux/GrapheneOS-related implementation. The technical staffing should avoid dependence on a single specialist. Assume the project's primary physical base is in Copenhagen, Denmark, at a Danish university, independent research institution, or security-qualified R&D facility providing access-controlled workspace appropriate for security-sensitive digital identity research, development, and stakeholder meetings. Budget for institutional overhead, external security review, and limited legal/procurement support explicitly rather than treating them as absorbed costs.

Success criteria: by month 24, the project has produced a published feasibility and risk report on platform-neutral and fallback-capable Danish digital identity; delivered two working non-production demonstrators (one mobile-oriented, one browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback); secured a formal written response from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or another relevant authority to the project's proposal; and achieved inclusion of platform-neutrality, fallback access, continuity, resilience, or supplier-concentration language in at least one official AltID-related design, policy, consultation, or procurement document. A stretch goal is that the project's EU-level engagement contributes to language in relevant European Digital Identity Wallet materials that supports platform-neutral access or certified fallback mechanisms. Do not use consumer adoption rates as a primary KPI for this project, because it is not shipping a production identity product.

Pick a realistic scenario that accounts for institutional inertia, regulatory friction, incumbent resistance, financing uncertainty, and the likelihood that convenience arguments will be used against platform-neutral alternatives. Do not assume constructive cooperation from existing operators. The primary lever is not technical disruption for its own sake, but the combination of resilience framing, procurement intervention, standards pressure, and credible technical demonstration.
